exercises.prompt: Build a CTE named vaccine_mon containing every Digimon whose attribute is 'Vaccine'. From it, return the name and release_year of the Vaccine Digimon that debuted in 1997, ordered by name ascending. Return the columns name and release_year.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT name, release_year FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine') SELECT name, release_year FROM vaccine_mon WHERE release_year = 1997 ORDER BY name
hints: Define the CTE with WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT ... WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine'), then write a normal SELECT that reads from vaccine_mon and filters to release_year = 1997.
WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT name, release_year FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine') SELECT name, release_year FROM vaccine_mon WHERE release_year = 1997 ORDER BY name.

```sql
WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT name, release_year FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine') SELECT name, release_year FROM vaccine_mon WHERE release_year = 1997 ORDER BY name
```

exercises.prompt: Using a CTE named by_level that counts how many Digimon sit at each level (ignore rows where level is NULL), return the level and its count as digimon_count for the levels that have at least 100 Digimon. Order by digimon_count descending, then level ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH by_level AS (SELECT level, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE level IS NOT NULL GROUP BY level) SELECT level, digimon_count FROM by_level WHERE digimon_count >= 100 ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, level
hints: Put the grouped count inside the CTE — SELECT level, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count ... GROUP BY level with level IS NOT NULL — then filter the CTE's rows to digimon_count >= 100 in the outer query.

WITH by_level AS (SELECT level, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE level IS NOT NULL GROUP BY level) SELECT level, digimon_count FROM by_level WHERE digimon_count >= 100 ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, level.

```sql
WITH by_level AS (SELECT level, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE level IS NOT NULL GROUP BY level) SELECT level, digimon_count FROM by_level WHERE digimon_count >= 100 ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, level
```

exercises.prompt: Build a CTE named armor_mon holding the id and name of every Armor-level Digimon. Join it to the evolutions table (armor_mon.id = evolutions.from_id) to list the unconditional evolutions (rows where condition IS NULL) that begin from an Armor-level Digimon. Return armor_name (the Armor Digimon's name) and to_name (the form it evolves into). Order by armor_name ascending, then to_name ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH armor_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE level = 'Armor') SELECT a.name AS armor_name, e.to_name FROM armor_mon a JOIN evolutions e ON a.id = e.from_id WHERE e.condition IS NULL ORDER BY a.name, e.to_name
hints: The CTE narrows digimon to the Armor level; then treat it like any table and JOIN it to evolutions on the id/from_id key, keeping only rows whose condition IS NULL.

WITH armor_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE level = 'Armor') SELECT a.name AS armor_name, e.to_name FROM armor_mon a JOIN evolutions e ON a.id = e.from_id WHERE e.condition IS NULL ORDER BY a.name, e.to_name.

```sql
WITH armor_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE level = 'Armor') SELECT a.name AS armor_name, e.to_name FROM armor_mon a JOIN evolutions e ON a.id = e.from_id WHERE e.condition IS NULL ORDER BY a.name, e.to_name
```

exercises.prompt: In two steps, find the attributes that are more common than average. First a CTE named attr_counts that counts the Digimon carrying each attribute (ignore rows where attribute is NULL). Then a CTE named avg_count that computes the average of those per-attribute counts. Return attribute and its count as digimon_count for the attributes whose count is strictly greater than that average, ordered by digimon_count descending, then attribute ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH attr_counts AS (SELECT attribute, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL GROUP BY attribute), avg_count AS (SELECT AVG(digimon_count) AS avg_n FROM attr_counts) SELECT attribute, digimon_count FROM attr_counts WHERE digimon_count > (SELECT avg_n FROM avg_count) ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, attribute
hints: Chain two CTEs: attr_counts holds the per-attribute counts, and avg_count reads from attr_counts to get the single average. Then compare each attribute's count to that average, pulled in as a scalar subquery.

WITH attr_counts AS (SELECT attribute, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL GROUP BY attribute), avg_count AS (SELECT AVG(digimon_count) AS avg_n FROM attr_counts) SELECT attribute, digimon_count FROM attr_counts WHERE digimon_count > (SELECT avg_n FROM avg_count) ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, attribute.

```sql
WITH attr_counts AS (SELECT attribute, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL GROUP BY attribute), avg_count AS (SELECT AVG(digimon_count) AS avg_n FROM attr_counts) SELECT attribute, digimon_count FROM attr_counts WHERE digimon_count > (SELECT avg_n FROM avg_count) ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, attribute
```

exercises.prompt: Build a CTE named type_stats that, for each type with at least 30 Digimon (ignore rows where type is NULL), holds the type, its Digimon count as digimon_count, and its average release_year rounded to 1 decimal place as avg_year. Return type, digimon_count, and avg_year, ordered by digimon_count descending, then type ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH type_stats AS (SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count, ROUND(AVG(release_year), 1) AS avg_year FROM digimon WHERE type IS NOT NULL GROUP BY type HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30) SELECT type, digimon_count, avg_year FROM type_stats ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, type
hints: Do the grouping, counting, averaging, and the at-least-30 cut all inside the CTE with GROUP BY type HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30; wrap the average in ROUND(AVG(release_year), 1) for one decimal place.

WITH type_stats AS (SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count, ROUND(AVG(release_year), 1) AS avg_year FROM digimon WHERE type IS NOT NULL GROUP BY type HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30) SELECT type, digimon_count, avg_year FROM type_stats ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, type.

```sql
WITH type_stats AS (SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count, ROUND(AVG(release_year), 1) AS avg_year FROM digimon WHERE type IS NOT NULL GROUP BY type HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30) SELECT type, digimon_count, avg_year FROM type_stats ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, type
```

exercises.prompt: Which Vaccine-attribute Digimon are the biggest evolution hubs? First a CTE named vaccine_mon with the id and name of every Digimon whose attribute is 'Vaccine'. Then a CTE named evo_counts that joins vaccine_mon to the evolutions table on id = from_id and, for each Vaccine Digimon, counts its distinct evolved forms (distinct to_id) as form_count, keeping only those with at least 60 distinct forms. Return name and form_count, ordered by form_count descending, then name ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine'), evo_counts AS (SELECT v.name, COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) AS form_count FROM vaccine_mon v JOIN evolutions e ON v.id = e.from_id GROUP BY v.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) >= 60) SELECT name, form_count FROM evo_counts ORDER BY form_count DESC, name
hints: Two CTEs: vaccine_mon restricts digimon to the Vaccine attribute, and evo_counts joins it to evolutions and groups by name so COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) is each hub's distinct-form count — filter that with HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) >= 60.

WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine'), evo_counts AS (SELECT v.name, COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) AS form_count FROM vaccine_mon v JOIN evolutions e ON v.id = e.from_id GROUP BY v.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) >= 60) SELECT name, form_count FROM evo_counts ORDER BY form_count DESC, name.

```sql
WITH vaccine_mon AS (SELECT id, name FROM digimon WHERE attribute = 'Vaccine'), evo_counts AS (SELECT v.name, COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) AS form_count FROM vaccine_mon v JOIN evolutions e ON v.id = e.from_id GROUP BY v.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.to_id) >= 60) SELECT name, form_count FROM evo_counts ORDER BY form_count DESC, name
```
